macro_segments.title: Introduction to Daf 22 and Review of Previous Discussion on Neveilah and Melikah

micro_segments.title: Opening review of Daf 22 and previous discussion on definitions of neveilah and death
Distinction between chatat ha-of and olat ha-of regarding separation of simanim
Tanna Kamma and Rabbi Yishmael position: fully separate both simanim without cutting skin
Rabbi Elazar b'Rabbi Shimon position: only majority of simanim separated

macro_segments.title: Gemara Discussion on Rabbi Elazar b'Rabbi Shimon's Position Regarding Head and Body Connection

micro_segments.title: Gemara's inquiry into Rabbi Elazar b'Rabbi Shimon's meaning regarding chatat ha-of with head and body together
Explanation of head held via body during blood squirting and connection of head and body
Gemara's challenge: why not say only one siman like chatat ha-of
Response distinguishing olat ha-of from chatat ha-of through the word ve-hikrivo

macro_segments.title: Tanna Kamma's Argument: How the Word Ve-hikrivo Frees the Olat Ha-of from Comparison to Chatat Ha-of

micro_segments.title: Tanna Kamma position on fully severing both simanim by olat ha-of
Gemara's question: why do we need ve-hikrivo if we already learn from u-malak ve-hiktir
Gemara's answer: without ve-hikrivo, one might learn that olat ha-of is compared to chatat ha-of
Alternative derivation from u-malak ve-hiktir: melikah must occur at the top of the mizbeach
The role of ve-hikrivo in severing the olat ha-of from association with chatat ha-of

macro_segments.title: Learning Laws of Bird Olat Ha-of from Animal Chatat: Source and Scope of Comparisons

micro_segments.title: Gemara's inquiry into what we learn about bird olat ha-of from animal chatat
Three things learned from animal chatat: non-maaser money, daytime offering, and use of right hand
Source from Aaron's sin offering on Yom Kippur that it must be from his own non-maaser money
Tosafot's explanation of why the verse about Aaron teaches a general principle beyond Yom Kippur

macro_segments.title: Daytime and Right-Hand Requirements: Derivation from General and Specific Sources

micro_segments.title: The requirement that offerings be brought during daytime from general verse about all korbanot
The requirement to use the right hand derived from animal chatat and the teaching of Rabba Bar Bar Chana on the word finger
Distinction: word finger signals right hand, but word kohen alone does not necessarily indicate right hand
Melikah of the bird olat ha-of must be performed with the right hand and using the kohen's fingernail

macro_segments.title: Source for Melikah from the Back of the Neck: Verbal Parallel Rather Than Categorical Link

micro_segments.title: Question on where Tanna Kamma and Rabbi Elazar b'Rabbi Shimon learn that melikah comes from back of neck
Answer: learning melikah from melikah through verbal analogy, not through categorical comparison

macro_segments.title: Introduction to Mutually Exclusive Categories: Shechitah and Melikah as Opposite Functions

micro_segments.title: Summary of the discussion: shechitah is from front and sides, melikah is from back only
Introduction to pattern of mishnas: what's good for A is not good for B and vice versa

macro_segments.title: Mishna on Turtledoves and Pigeons: Age Requirements and the Excluded Middle Category of Adolescence

micro_segments.title: Mishna: what's kosher for turtledoves (adults) is invalid for young pigeons; vice versa
The beginning of golden coloring of neck feathers: invalid for both adult and young categories
Tanna's teaching: turtledoves require adults, young pigeons require children
Attempted kal vachomer: if young pigeons are kosher, shouldn't young turtledoves also be kosher
Response: the word bnei yona limits young pigeons to children, thus cannot extend to turtledoves

macro_segments.title: Derivation from Silence: How the Consistent Phrasing of Verses Establishes Exclusive Age Categories

micro_segments.title: Rava's principle: the Torah never reverses the terms, always saying torim and bnei yona in the same order
Lo lishtameit kra: the phrase would not have omitted this if it were not the case
Argument from silence applied to halakha: absence of evidence for two-thousand years creates expectation of presence
Parallel deriving exclusive categories through consistent usage rather than explicit exclusion

macro_segments.title: Limiting the Kal Vachomer Through Parallel Derivation: Both Categories Are Exclusive

micro_segments.title: Gemara questions: how do we know torim doesn't include young ones as a generic term
Response: through parallel derivation from bnei yona; what applies to one category applies in reverse to the other

macro_segments.title: The Excluded Middle: Adolescence as Either Its Own Category or a Doubt Between Adult and Child

micro_segments.title: Setting: person vows to bring either a turtledove or young pigeon, brings both in the adolescent stage
First possibility: adolescence is its own category, so person did not fulfill vow (neither adult nor child)
Second possibility: adolescence is a doubt between categories; one of the animals must be correct
Rava's argument: the need for an explicit verse to exclude adolescence proves it is its own category
If it were merely a doubt, no verse would be needed, as we don't bring doubtful korbanot
Gemara's response: the verse is only an asmachta; its real purpose is to exclude other defects

macro_segments.title: Broader Implications: The Question of Dichotomy and Middle Categories Across Halakha

micro_segments.title: Application of safek versus biryah question to other cases: androgynos, bein hashmashot, and pflugei
Philosophical question: does something undefined fall into existing categories or create its own
Principle that the answer may differ depending on the context and subject matter
